---
bibliography: [refs.bib]
---

# Introduction

Universities and other higher education institutions are central to producing and disseminating knowledge, contributing to human capital development. Instructors' choices -- how to teach, which materials to assign, and whether to collaborate with colleagues -- shape students' educational experience, influencing their learning outcomes and career trajectories substantially [@bakkenes2010teacher;@vembye2024effects;@han2023interdisciplinary]. These choices reflect both individual preferences and institutional constraints, which may not always align with students' learning needs or societal objectives. For instance, instructors may prefer to work alone or collaborate with colleagues with similar backgrounds, which can limit course diversity or interdisciplinarity and reinforce gender stereotypes in specific subjects [@ellemers2018gender;@lockwood2006someone;@canaan2023impact]. Collaboration can also advance instructors' professional development by exposing them to different pedagogical approaches or teaching methods [@buckingham2021learning]. Yet, despite their significance, the factors shaping these teaching choices remain poorly understood, primarily due to a lack of data.

This study addresses this gap by analysing a large dataset of English-language course syllabi from 1990 to 2019 across fifteen countries and from 2019 to 2023 across thirty-three European countries. These data, that have been previously used in research on school curricula [@han2023interdisciplinary], teaching metrics [@nowogrodzki2016mining], the impacts of AI [@gao2023quantifying], and the education-innovation gap [@biasi2022education], enable a systematic, quantitative analysis of long-term trends. Each syllabus includes the instructor's gender, institutional affiliation, course field, assigned readings, and a course description. This information allows us to examine gender-based team formation, revealing potential preferences and institutional constraints in teaching collaborations. Furthermore, we analyse how team composition, particularly gender and size, relates to teaching practices, such as interdisciplinarity and reading assignments, while controlling for institutional factors, field, academic year, and other relevant variables.

<!-- Our work contributes links with four strands of literature. Research shows that gender and organisational constraints shape academic team formation [e.g., @guimera2005team; @wuchty2007increasing; @milojevic2014principles; @yang2022gender], but it is unclear whether the same applies to teaching teams. Second, instructor gender can influence the representation of women scholars in citations and course readings [e.g., @dion2018gendered], but it is unclear whether mixed-gender teams may offset this effect. Third, mixed-gender teams have been linked to greater interdisciplinarity in research [e.g., @pinheiro2022women], though evidence in teaching contexts is limited. Finally, gender-diverse teams tend to adopt more novel or unconventional approaches [e.g., @yang2022gender], a pattern we explore in the context of pedagogical innovation.  -->

We investigate three central questions: (i) how gender and institutional constraints are associated with the formation of teaching teams; (ii) whether instructor gender is associated with the inclusion of female-authored readings; and (iii) whether mixed-gender teams are linked to greater interdisciplinarity in course content and with the adoption of novel or unconventional readings, including materials that are recent or at the frontier of research ("scientific novelty"). Previous research shows that these teaching choices shape students' learning outcomes and career trajectories [@bakkenes2010teacher; @vembye2024effects; @han2023interdisciplinary], and they can also influence instructors' professional development and promotion within their institutions [@buckingham2021learning].

We complement these analyses with an exploration of the formation of teaching teams pre- and post- COVID-19 in Europe. The COVID-19 pandemic disrupted academic work in many countries, especially for women and caregivers [e.g., @myers2020unequal], reshaped collaboration networks, and accelerated the shift to online and hybrid teaching. The shift to online and hybrid teaching may also have affected team formation across gender and disciplines. Using COVID-19 as an exogenous shock, we study changes in the prevalence and gender composition of teaching teams around 2020.

Our results reveal a significant role for gender in co-teaching practices, with mixed-gender teams occurring consistently less frequently than same-gender teams across institutions and fields. Furthermore, mixed-gender teams arise much less often than expected under a null model that forms teams in a gender-neutral manner while holding field-related and institutional constraints fixed. These findings underscore a persistent and widespread underrepresentation of mixed-gender collaborations in teaching not explained by unobserved differences across fields or institutions. 

Our analysis further reveals a significant and strong association between the gender composition of a teaching team and the course's interdisciplinarity, with mixed-gender teams being more interdisciplinary than all-male teams or courses taught by individual instructors. This evidence suggests that gender diversity can be a potential lever for increasing interdisciplinary teaching in tertiary education. 

We also find a significant association between team configurations and the fraction of cited female authors. Specifically, classes taught by a female instructor alone tend to cite a higher fraction of female authors than those taught by male instructors, with mixed-gender teams falling in between. This finding highlights teaching practices as an understudied potential driver of women's underrepresentation in academia. Finally, we observe a trend toward novelty: female instructors assign more recent readings than their male counterparts, regardless of team size, but mixed-gender teams tend to cite more conventional readings and are less likely to cite cutting-edge research. This evidence contributes to the debate on the factors shaping the education-innovation gap [@biasi2022education].
